title: The Lean Startup / Minimum Viable Product
question: What is the actual purpose of a Minimum Viable Product, according to Lean Startup thinking?
correctOption: To run the fastest, cheapest valid experiment that tests a core business assumption

title: How to Get Startup Ideas
question: According to Paul Graham, why do the best startup ideas often sound bad at first?
correctOption: Because the market for them doesn't obviously exist yet — they're noticed early, before demand is visible to everyone

title: 1,000 True Fans
question: What does Kevin Kelly's '1,000 True Fans' model actually depend on to work economically?
correctOption: Low-cost direct distribution and payment, so serving a small, deeply loyal audience becomes profitable on its own

title: Competition Is for Losers
question: Why does Peter Thiel argue that intense competition is actually bad for a company's long-term prospects?
correctOption: Because it drives margins toward zero, leaving little capital to reinvest in the long term

title: All Markets Are Not Created Equal (Note to Self on TAM)
question: What is Bill Gurley's central warning about using TAM (Total Addressable Market) to judge a startup?
correctOption: A large TAM number alone says little; the market's underlying structure (frequency, consolidation tendency, supply/demand cost) determines whether it can produce a durable, valuable winner

title: SaaS Metrics 2.0
question: Why is customer churn especially dangerous in a SaaS/subscription business, more than in a one-time-purchase business?
correctOption: Because revenue arrives gradually over time, so if a customer leaves before you've earned back what it cost to acquire them, that cost is never recovered

title: Blitzscaling
question: Under what condition does 'blitzscaling' — deliberately sacrificing efficiency for speed — actually make strategic sense?
correctOption: In winner-take-most markets, where being first lets you build a compounding, durable advantage (network effects, data, brand) that's hard for slower competitors to overcome

title: Aggregation Theory
question: According to Ben Thompson's Aggregation Theory, why did the internet shift power from suppliers to platforms that don't even own the supply?
correctOption: Because distribution became nearly free online, so the scarce resource shifted to user attention/relationship, which aggregators capture and then dictate terms to suppliers

title: Invisible Asymptotes
question: What does Eugene Wei mean by an 'invisible asymptote' in a growing business?
correctOption: A structural, mechanical limit built into how the business currently works, which stays hidden in the growth curve until the business actually hits it

title: The New Moats
question: Why does Jerry Chen argue that a 'system of intelligence' is a stronger moat than older forms of competitive advantage?
correctOption: Because it compounds over time through a data flywheel — usage generates data, data improves the product, a better product attracts more usage — which a copycat with no usage history can't quickly replicate

title: The Rise of the Solo Capitalists
question: What structural advantage does a 'solo capitalist' typically have over a traditional multi-partner VC firm when competing for a deal?
correctOption: They can decide and commit quickly since there's no partner committee to convince, and founders get a direct relationship with the actual decision-maker

title: Fairchild Semiconductor Founding
question: What made the Fairchild deal structurally different from a normal stock purchase, and why did that matter?
correctOption: Fairchild Camera and Instrument got an option to buy the entire new company later, rather than just purchasing shares upfront — letting founders keep control while proving the business worked

title: A Proposal to American Research and Development
question: Why did Olsen and Anderson pitch selling circuit modules before pitching interactive computers?
correctOption: The computer business had a reputation as an investor money-loser at the time, so a smaller proven product de-risked the pitch and built credibility toward the bigger ambition

title: YouTube Investment Memo
question: What did Roelof Botha's YouTube investment memo prioritize as evidence the company was worth backing, despite having little revenue?
correctOption: The explosive volume and growth rate of user-generated video uploads, as a leading indicator of becoming the default place for online video

title: Facebook (thefacebook) Media Kit / Early Pitch
question: What was the core argument of Facebook's 2004 media kit pitch to advertisers?
correctOption: That dense, repeated engagement from a narrow, targetable audience was more valuable to advertisers than a larger but less engaged audience

title: UberCab Pitch Deck
question: How did Kalanick and Camp initially position UberCab in their 2008 pitch deck?
correctOption: As a premium, invite-only black-car service comparable to NetJets, aimed at professionals rather than the general public

title: Airbnb Series A Pitch Deck
question: Why did the bootstrapped 'cereal box' story matter to Airbnb's fundraising, beyond being a memorable anecdote?
correctOption: It demonstrated the founders' resourcefulness and execution under extreme constraint, which mattered for trust during a financial crisis when the core idea itself looked unproven

title: The Secret Tesla Motors Master Plan (just between you and me)
question: Why did Tesla's master plan start with an expensive, low-volume sports car instead of an affordable mass-market car?
correctOption: The expensive early car funded and proved out the technology, manufacturing capability, and credibility needed to eventually build cheaper, higher-volume cars

title: The Pricing of Options and Corporate Liabilities
question: Why doesn't the Black-Scholes formula require knowing an investor's risk tolerance or the stock's expected future return?
correctOption: Because it prices the option via a riskless hedge whose return must equal the risk-free rate regardless of investor preferences — only volatility, not expected return, matters

title: A Mathematical Theory of Communication
question: What was radical about Shannon's approach to defining 'information'?
correctOption: He measured information purely by how surprising/unpredictable it was (its entropy), deliberately ignoring the actual meaning or content of the message

title: Information Management: A Proposal
question: Why did Berners-Lee's hypertext system deliberately avoid having a central index of all links?
correctOption: A central index would have required someone's permission or registration for every link, creating a bottleneck; without one, the system could scale without a single point of failure, at the cost of links sometimes breaking

title: The Anatomy of a Large-Scale Hypertextual Web Search Engine
question: Why is PageRank harder to manipulate than simple keyword-frequency ranking?
correctOption: Because a page's rank depends on being linked to by other already-trusted pages, not just on what words appear on the page itself — which is much harder to fake than stuffing keywords

title: The Internet Tidal Wave
question: How did Microsoft respond to the threat it identified from Netscape in the Internet Tidal Wave memo?
correctOption: It bundled a free web browser (Internet Explorer) into Windows, using its OS monopoly to undercut Netscape's paid browser business model

title: Increasing Returns and the New World of Business
question: According to W. Brian Arthur, why do knowledge-based technology markets tend toward winner-take-all outcomes?
correctOption: Because high upfront costs combined with near-zero reproduction costs, network effects, and switching costs create a feedback loop where an early lead compounds into a further lead, unlike traditional industries with diminishing returns

title: Software 2.0
question: According to Karpathy, what makes up the 'source code' of a Software 2.0 system?
correctOption: The neural network's architecture and the dataset used to train it — since the actual logic is encoded as learned numerical weights, not hand-written instructions

title: Efficient Estimation of Word Representations (word2vec)
question: How does word2vec end up capturing semantic relationships between words, if it's only trained to predict nearby words?
correctOption: The prediction task forces the network to build an internal vector representation where words used in similar contexts land near each other, and consistent relationships emerge as consistent directions in that space

title: ImageNet Classification with Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (AlexNet)
question: What combination of techniques allowed AlexNet to train a deep network fast enough to be practical in 2012?
correctOption: The ReLU activation function (faster training than sigmoid), dropout (reduces overfitting), and splitting the model across two GPUs to handle its 60 million parameters

title: Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition (ResNet)
question: What problem did ResNet's 'skip connections' specifically solve?
correctOption: They let the training signal (gradient) bypass layers when needed, solving the vanishing gradient problem that made very deep networks degrade in accuracy rather than improve

title: Attention Is All You Need
question: What made Transformers more practical to train at massive scale than the recurrent models that came before them?
correctOption: Self-attention lets the model process all words in a sequence simultaneously rather than one at a time, which parallelizes far better on GPU hardware than sequential recurrent processing

title: Language Models are Few-Shot Learners (GPT-3)
question: What is 'in-context learning,' as demonstrated by GPT-3?
correctOption: Performing a new task correctly just from a description or a few examples shown in the prompt itself, without any weight updates or retraining

title: Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models
question: What specific guidance from the original Scaling Laws paper was later overturned by DeepMind's Chinchilla paper?
correctOption: The recommendation that, for a fixed compute budget, model size should be scaled up much faster than training data — Chinchilla showed the two should scale together

title: Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models (Chinchilla)
question: What did the Chinchilla paper prove was wrong with prior industry practice in training large language models?
correctOption: Models were being built too large relative to how much training data they were given — a smaller model trained on proportionally more data outperformed a much larger, undertrained one

title: DeepSeek-R1: Incentivizing Reasoning Capability via RL
question: What training approach did DeepSeek-R1 rely on primarily to teach the model to reason, rather than mainly using human-labeled reasoning examples?
correctOption: Reinforcement learning with rule-based rewards (correct answers, consistent language) building on a small amount of stabilizing 'cold-start' data

title: The Bitter Lesson
question: According to Rich Sutton's 'Bitter Lesson,' why do general, compute-heavy methods tend to eventually beat human-engineered domain expertise in AI?
correctOption: Because hand-engineered knowledge imposes a hard ceiling on performance, while general methods (search and learning) can keep improving indefinitely as computation gets cheaper over time

title: Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System
question: How does Bitcoin prevent double-spending without a central trusted authority like a bank?
correctOption: Proof-of-work makes rewriting transaction history computationally expensive — an attacker would need to out-compute the majority of the network's processing power to build a longer alternate chain

title: The Bullish Case for Bitcoin
question: According to Boyapati's framework, why is it backwards to judge Bitcoin as a failure for not yet being widely used to buy everyday goods?
correctOption: Because becoming a widely-used medium of exchange historically requires first becoming a trusted store of value — Bitcoin was still in that earlier stage, and skipping ahead isn't how monetization has historically worked

title: Ethereum Whitepaper: A Next-Generation Smart Contract Platform
question: Why does Ethereum require paying 'gas' fees for every computation, unlike Bitcoin's simpler transaction model?
correctOption: Because Ethereum allows unrestricted, Turing-complete computation, and gas fees (scaling with computational complexity) prevent infinite loops or spam from freezing the shared network

title: Fat Protocols
question: Why does Joel Monegro argue that value in crypto accrues to the protocol layer rather than the application layer, unlike the internet?
correctOption: Because blockchain data is shared and open rather than proprietary, weakening individual applications' moats, while the base protocol token captures value as the whole ecosystem built on it grows

title: Uniswap Whitepaper (Automated Market Maker)
question: How does Uniswap determine the price of a trade, given that there's no order book matching buyers and sellers?
correctOption: A smart contract holds a pool of two tokens and enforces that their product stays constant (x*y=k); trading against the pool shifts the ratio, and price is derived automatically from that formula

title: Solana: A New Architecture for a High Performance Blockchain
question: What problem does Solana's Proof of History primarily solve?
correctOption: It creates a verifiable, decentralized way to prove the order and timing of events, cutting the communication overhead nodes would otherwise need to agree on ordering

title: Why Decentralization Matters
question: According to Chris Dixon, why do centralized platforms tend to become adversarial toward the developers and users who helped them grow?
correctOption: Because once a platform dominates its market, its incentives shift from courting an ecosystem to extracting value from it, and there's no structural mechanism stopping that shift the way there is with decentralized protocols

title: IPO 2.0 / SPAC Investor Letters
question: What specific advantage did Palihapitiya claim SPACs had over traditional IPOs?
correctOption: SPACs let companies share forward-looking financial projections (restricted in traditional IPOs) and bypass the underwriter-controlled roadshow, giving retail investors earlier and more direct access

title: Inversion
question: You want to build a lasting friendship. Inversion suggests asking:
correctOption: What are ten ways to guarantee this friendship dies?

title: Second-Order Thinking
question: A CEO cuts prices to gain market share. First-order effect: sales rise. What's the classic missed second-order effect?
correctOption: Competitors match, margins fall for everyone, and customers now expect the lower price.

title: Circle of Competence
question: What does Buffett say matters most about the circle of competence?
correctOption: Knowing exactly where it ends.

title: Hanlon's Razor
question: A coworker leaves you out of a meeting invite. Hanlon's Razor prompts you to first suspect:
correctOption: They forgot or didn't realize you needed to be included.

title: Compounding
question: Why do most people underestimate compounding?
correctOption: The early years look linear and boring, so people quit before the curve bends.

title: Lindy Effect
question: Applying Lindy to technology choice, which is a safer long-term bet, all else equal?
correctOption: SQL, which has been production-critical for 50 years.

title: Path Dependence
question: Which is the best example of path dependence?
correctOption: Choosing a programming language early in a codebase and being unable to reasonably switch a decade later.

title: Optionality
question: Why is optionality often undervalued?
correctOption: Because people prefer certainty and pay too little for the right to act later in a volatile world.

title: Expected Value
question: A bet has 90% chance of losing $10 and 10% chance of winning $200. What's the EV?

title: Pre-Mortem
question: Why does a pre-mortem surface more failure modes than a normal risk review?
correctOption: Imagining the failure has already occurred lets people describe causes without seeming disloyal or negative.

title: Base Rates
question: A founder says 'our startup will succeed because our team is unusually talented.' What is the base-rates critique?
correctOption: Most founders think their team is unusually talented, and most startups still fail. Start with the outside view and adjust modestly.

title: Regret Minimization
question: Regret minimization mostly protects against which asymmetry?
correctOption: That short-term risks feel large while long-term inaction quietly compounds into the biggest regret.

title: Goodhart's Law
question: A support team is measured only on 'tickets closed per day.' What's the classic Goodhart failure?
correctOption: Agents close tickets quickly without solving problems, hurting customers but hitting the number.

title: Chesterton's Fence
question: A new engineer wants to delete a mysterious cron job no one documents. Chesterton's Fence says:
correctOption: Find out why it exists first; the surprising cost of removal is often invisible.

title: Principal–Agent Problem
question: Why do publicly traded companies use stock-based executive compensation?
correctOption: To align the agent's incentives with the principal's long-term outcome.

title: Cobra Effect
question: A city pays residents per pound of trash collected. What's the likely cobra outcome?
correctOption: People bring in trash from neighboring cities to collect payment.

title: Deep Work
question: Deep work is best trained by:
correctOption: Scheduling protected time and building tolerance for the discomfort of undistracted work.

title: Taste
question: According to PG, the fastest way to develop taste is to:
correctOption: Consume the best work in your field and reverse-engineer why it works.
